item.title: Comment on Strong vs Weak Reference in Cocoa by Memory management in Swift: Strong Reference & Retain Cycle – codewithshabib
item.dc:creator: Memory management in Swift: Strong Reference & Retain Cycle – codewithshabib
item.description: […] ARC tracks the number of references to a object created and deallocates the instance when no longer needed. But it is possible to achieve a situation where an instance of a class never reaches to a point where it has no strong references. […]
item.content:encoded: […] ARC tracks the number of references to a object created and deallocates the instance when no longer needed. But it is possible to achieve a situation where an instance of a class never reaches to a point where it has no strong references. […]

item.title: Comment on Creating your own COCOAPOD by Ahmad Shabibul Hossain
item.dc:creator: Ahmad Shabibul Hossain
item.description:
In reply to <a href="./../../creating-your-own-cocoapod/comment-page-1/index.html#comment-17">Md. Aminul Islam Bhuiyan</a>.
Thanks! I was confused too, but when you get start using cocoapods you will see the difference by yourself. It is fairly easy to use and you don't have to worry a single thing about dependency complications (You know how messy that can get over the time!).
item.content:encoded:
In reply to Md. Aminul Islam Bhuiyan.

Thanks! I was confused too, but when you get start using cocoapods you will see the difference by yourself. It is fairly easy to use and you don’t have to worry a single thing about dependency complications (You know how messy that can get over the time!).

item.title: Comment on Creating your own COCOAPOD by Ahmad Shabibul Hossain
item.dc:creator: Ahmad Shabibul Hossain
item.description:
In reply to <a href="./../../creating-your-own-cocoapod/comment-page-1/index.html#comment-18">Mehedi Hasan</a>.
Thanks man :)
